titleStmt.title: Letter from B. Abraham to the Chairman, General Prisons Board, 19 April 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Benjamin Abraham

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Benjamin Abraham (b. 1965), Clerk of Works for General Prisons Board, to the Chairman, General Prisons Board. This letter forms part of a large volume of correspondence between the Mountjoy Prison authorities and N. C. Laundry Ltd. concerning a dispute over responsibility for the collapse of a wall between the two properties and a claim by the laundry for a contribution towards reconstruction.In this letter Abraham gives estimates of the cost of reconstruction of the wall to its original height and also an alternative and less expensive construction of a wooden 6 ft fence.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/7190

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
1483 General Prison's Board Dublin Castle 19 th April 1916
Mountjoy Prison
Damage to wall Chairman I beg to state that the
Estimated cost of rebuilding to
its original height the damaged
portion of the wall between the
N. C. Laundry Yards Mt Joy
Prison Grounds would be Â£116.12.6 To provide and erect a
substantial wooden fence say
6tf high would cost Â£38.10.9 B. Abraham
Under Secretary
Submitted in reply to
minute of 15.4.1916
Max S. Green
20.4.16
Under Secretary
Submitted in reply to your
minute of the 14 th inst.
I.J.T
20.416
I. Rec. from cate U.S. 5/5
II. Spoke to Mr. HeadCon.
III. Pending Treasury reply.
I.J.T.
13.v.16
NORTH CIRCULAR ROAD (TOP OF BERKLEY STREET). DUBLIN, April 10th 1916 C Munro Esq H. M Prison Mountjoy Dublin Dear Sir We duly received yours 5 th and
we are pleased to know that something
may be done about our wall but the
erection of a wire fence we are very
much afraid does not quite meet the
situation and we feel certain would
be unsatisfactory to both you and
ourselves. We suggest your sending
us a contribution towards cost
of the wall and we shall then have
a close fence built at our expense
If you fix a reasonable sum we
will not be hard to satisfy. Yours faithfully N.C. L.D.
From the GOVERNOR H. M. PRISON Mountjoy, Dublin 5th April 1916 To Messrs the North City Laundry Coy. North Circular Road, Dublin. With to previous corresponence relative to the fallen
wall I am to enquire whether your Company would offer any objection to the
erection of a wire fence at public expense in lieu of the proposal to replace
the fallen wall. CTMunro Governor.
NORTH CIRCULAR ROAD (TOP OF BERKLEY STREET) DUBLIN 29th Mar 1916. Chief Crown Solicitor's Office, Dublin Castle, DUBLIN Dear Sirs We have been expecting to hear from you re our wall
tumbled by your ivy. As we are anxious to have this matter disâ
posed of and if possible without unpleasantness, we should be
glad if you would let us have your decision as soon as possible. Yours faithfully N. C. LAUNDRY, LTD
30th March 1916
re Alleged damage to Wall Dear Sirs I am in receipt of your letter of 29th March which I
have forwarded for the necessary instructions and shall communicate
with you immediately on receipt of same. Yours truly A Percy Lynch Asst. C.C.S.S Messrs N.C. Laundry Ltd., N.C. Road, Dublin.
